# Can AI Make Us Laugh? Comparing Jokes Generated by Witscript and a Human Expert

**Joe Toplyn, Ori Amir** — CHum 2025 · Guide entry #17 (companion) (Part 2 - Generating Jokes)

[paper page](https://aclanthology.org/2025.chum-1.8/) · [local PDF](../pdfs/x08-can-ai-make-us-laugh.pdf) · [full markdown](../md/x08-can-ai-make-us-laugh/x08-can-ai-make-us-laugh.md) · [extract](../extracts/x08-can-ai-make-us-laugh.json)

## TL;DR
This paper evaluates whether Witscript, a neural-symbolic AI joke-writing system, can write jokes as funny as those by a professional human comedy writer. Instead of using crowd ratings, it measures live audience laughter during standup sets and finds that AI-written jokes elicited laughter in the same range as human-written jokes; the funniest joke in Set 1 was AI-written, with Total Laughter = 241.

## Problem & Motivation
The authors argue that the central question for joke-generating AI is whether its jokes are as funny as human-crafted jokes. Prior work usually asks human evaluators, often non-experts on crowdsourcing platforms, to assign numerical funniness ratings. The paper argues that this is limited because jokes are designed to elicit laughter, not high numerical ratings. Since laughter is socially influenced and strongest in person, the authors propose live standup performance as a more direct and naturalistic evaluation setting.

## Approach
Witscript is described as a neural-symbolic hybrid system for American English. It uses human-designed joke-writing algorithms and calls a GPT-family OpenAI large language model to execute those algorithms and other joke-production methods. The study compares Witscript with a human expert: a longtime joke writer for a well-known U.S. late-night comedy/talk show.

The authors introduce a metric called Total Laughter. They label laughter segments in audio recordings, convert the sound wave to decibels over time using dB = 20 * log10(|s| + 1e^{-6}), and compute the area under the curve using Simpson’s numerical integration method. Total Laughter is reported in decibel-seconds.

## Data & Experimental Setup
Author OA selected 16 current news headlines. Author JT removed headlines tied to events after Witscript’s model knowledge cutoff, then selected eight headlines judged suitable for joke writing and edited each into a one-sentence topic. Witscript and the human expert independently wrote jokes for the eight topics over three days. Each source selected one punchline per topic, yielding 16 punchlines.

Experienced standup comics performed two analyzed sets in U.S. comedy venues. The comics did not know which jokes were AI-written. Each set contained all eight topics, with half the punchlines by Witscript and half by the human expert; topic order and source assignment were randomized and counterbalanced. The analyzed sets were performed by Mike Perkins at the same North Hollywood venue, with audience sizes of 35 and 15. Two other sets were excluded because of poor venue quality or small audience size (N<10).

## Results
In Set 1, three of the four AI jokes elicited more laughter than at least one human joke, and the top joke was AI-written: Total Laughter = 241. On average, AI was slightly ahead in Set 1: AI M = 106, SD = 96; Human M = 104, SD = 86. This difference was not significant: Mann-Whitney U(4,4) = 8.0, ns.

In Set 2, the human writer led on average: AI M = 66, SD = 21; Human M = 99, SD = 93. This was also not significant: Mann-Whitney U(4,4) = 8.0, ns. Across sets, audience size differed (35 vs. 15), with longer laugh times in Set 1 (M = 2.16 sec. vs. 1.71 sec.) and higher Total Laughter (M = 105 vs. 83), but Median Laughter Loudness was identical (M = 48 for both sets). Topic-level comparisons found no significant AI-human differences for Total Laughter, Mean Loudness, Median Loudness, or Length of Laugh: all paired t values < 1, ns; a GLM controlling for Set effects returned the same result.

## Takeaways
- Live audience laughter can be used as a direct evaluation signal for joke generation.
- In this small study, Witscript performed within the range of a professional human comedy writer.
- Builders of humor systems should consider evaluation settings that resemble real use, not only numerical ratings.
- The results support the authors’ claim that comedy/talk show monologue-style joke generation for an American audience is not AI-complete.

## Limitations & Caveats
The study is small, and the authors state that the lack of statistical difference is not meaningful with the present sample size. Performance factors such as delivery, gestures, and facial expressions may affect laughter, though the comedian was blind to source. Audience members were not fully independent because laughter is contagious. Both Witscript and human submissions were cherry-picked from multiple candidates.
